content.claim: Governance centralization since 2013 (establishment of KLIK) has concentrated secondary-education decisions at central level while delegating pedagogical autonomy to schools, creating institutional bottlenecks rather than streamlining implementation capacity.
content.context: Fresh live research through the Implementation planning lens
limitations: 'Dysfunctional centralization' is characterized as independent commentary, not peer-reviewed institutional analysis

Teacher shortage is documented as structural (low pay, high student-teacher ratios, non-teaching duties), but causal link between centralization and shortage is inferred rather than directly established
No counterfactual comparison with pre-2013 governance outcomes provided
content.population: Population described in the cited finding
content.time_scope: As reported by the cited source
